item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image has been removed due to not being compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Turned out that I found guys just as much fun to swallow as gals.

Me too buddy, me too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do want to echo the praise this has gotten regarding the subtle world building. It is absolutely incredible how it blends in so well with the dialogue. The dialogue itself is also fantastic and the character is even more enjoyable. Just an amazing article overall and a wonderful example of how to do an SCP with a simplistic effect.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. The author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

Alright, another HF article without an actual link to the HF hub, which ultimately is one of the easiest and least invasive crosslinks to make. Combining that with the already existing link to the Dread and Circuses hub later in the article, and anyone wanting to find out more about HFCotD will have no shortage of wikiwalking to do. As such, I am suggesting the following:

Editing this:

SCP-2094 was recovered in an open field near Kamifurano, Japan, along with an assortment of non-anomalous artifacts related to GoI-233 (Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting). SCP-2094 was discovered bound in chains and locked inside a large antique trunk bearing the words "FOR ESSIE" written on the top panel in red paint.

To this:

SCP-2094 was recovered in an open field near Kamifurano, Japan, along with an assortment of non-anomalous artifacts related to GoI-233 (Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting). SCP-2094 was discovered bound in chains and locked inside a large antique trunk bearing the words "FOR ESSIE" written on the top panel in red paint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for a goddamn great story. Knott's Berry Farm reference got me hooked lol :')

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, it's Michael Keaton in full Beetlejuice mode, with a dash of Krusty the Klown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who read SCP-2094 interviews and heard Mark Hamil's voice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really loved this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was so ready to dislike this skip, only really started reading it because I'm going through all the HFCOTD skips and tales today but my word if you didn't keep me hooked. He was very well written, funny without being too wacky, you could really hear his voice while reading, and I was really starting to… ugh… care for him by the end.

Top notch.
All of PeppersGhost's original Herman Fuller SCP's are now at least +100, except this, which is +99. Let's get the to +100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was on the verge of shedding tears for Mr. Motormouth ;; It's hard for me to even like clowns/clown skips, let alone sympathize with them. And as someone who hasn't read a Herman Fuller piece before, this gets me very much interested in them and wanting to learn more about the mythos and story behind it all. Great goddamn job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the Calvin and Hobbes reference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
for all the work you did: +1
for the funny image: +1
sorry but i have only one account, total value: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading that part, that was almost exactly what popped into my head. Always loved Calvin & Hobbes. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please right a million tales, narrated by him. This is officially my new favorite SCP, which makes you my new favorite contributor. Can I have your autograph?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The trope in question is named for the Calvin and Hobbes version of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this article. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can we have a log of researchers going in his stomach and wandering around? Oh ya +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: actually it is a tvtropes refference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably one of my new favourite skips on this site! This is so good at making you care about this character! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. You can tell there was a lot of thought behind it. At first I saw the page and thought, "Ugh, scary clown. It's going to be an It." Then I read the description and I thought, "Okay, this is going to be squicky." Then I saw the huge interviews and thought, "This is going to be drawn out and completely uninteresting." I was set on a downvote.

Then I got into the article. He's creepy, but fun and engaging. I can't help but read his voice like Yakko from Animaniacs. The squickiest thing was the guy who turns himself inside out, and that's barely touched on. And the interviews really conveyed his character, as well as the characters of the first researcher, being very no-nonsense (as per Foundation norm) and the psychiatrist (a nice inversion to your typical Foundation employee). I like the slightly less cold side of the Foundation that's shown. There's a blurred line there as to whether the Foundation wants him better to get more secrets, or if they earnestly want to see him better for his own sake. I'm also really intrigued about the circus. Is it frightening? Malevolent? Or is it like your usual circus, trying to entertain and thrive in spite of an ever more scrutinizing audience and dwindling interest? I'll definitely be reading more about them.

That being said, I think the article starts losing its tone a lot towards the end of the piece. About where the warm fuzzies set in that I liked from the shrink. A little contradictory, but it's what I got. It kinda feels like it gets too personal at the end. Overall, I like it, its a great article, but I think it would strengthen it as a whole if the clinical tone kept a bit better.

Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent interview logs. This character was very interesting and completely believable. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You didn't use my idea :(

Still, this is a solid +1. Very nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to imply that this guy's got a million stories waiting to be told. And they may still be heard later on in tale form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think that the reason Manny went to such lengths to bring the girl to the Circus was because she reminded him if someone he missed, an act that had ended a long time ago…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for all the things.

Aww…Elisa. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few problems with this piece. The most obvious one is the age inconsistencies. He's 38 today, then he's 24 in 2006, stuff like that. Maybe just say his age at the time of recovery to leave out confusion.

Nice catch. I had meant to remove that line about his age. We have enough twenty-somethings already.

Second, the incident and interview logs, despite mostly being what makes this work, are too long for me. They'd work better on a separate page as a supplement.

If your problem is with the length, I don't see how splitting the piece into two different webpages would solve the issue. It's already broken up into clearly separated segments for manageability. As you pointed out, the supplementary material is (by design) the thing that supports the article, so splitting it off would be counterintuitive in this case. It's not just bonus material, it's an integral part of the story I'm trying to tell.

Now, you're welcome to say that the length of the problem if the piece is boring, but if you think the addenda are 'what makes this work,' I don't see why the length in itself should be an issue.

Still, I appreciate the feedback and look forward to hearing what else you have to say. Glad you enjoyed it enough to upvote, too. For the record, there's no need to put a disclaimer on your posts when leaving feedback. You're wholly entitled to voice your opinion on an article. Of course, other users are equally entitled to give their opinions on your opinions. That's how a creative writing community should be, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few problems with this piece. The most obvious one is the age inconsistencies. He's 38 today, then he's 24 in 2006, stuff like that. Maybe just say his age at the time of recovery to leave out confusion.
Second, the incident and interview logs, despite mostly being what makes this work, are too long for me. They'd work better on a separate page as a supplement.
There were some other things going on that I didn't like that I'll need to re-review on a desktop computer instead of a phone.

That being said, I did enjoy reading this, and, through whatever voodoo that tells you who upvoted what, you'll find an upvote from me.

Additional Note: I've never done something as awesome as invent a GOI (or anything, really), so don't take my advice as seriously as that of a veteran of this wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, basically the SCP is Kirby?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I'd get to know where did that submachine gun come from. Still nice story though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I particularly like about this SCP isn't the SCP itself(although it's neat), but all the little implications you've sprinkled into the story, from the thought that there are hundreds of GoIs to to the possibility that Motormouth is actually waxing poetic about the Circus out of fear. They really make the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome work, Peppersghost! +1 *\(^_^)/*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really, really nice one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I looked back at the length of the article after I read it and it didn't feel anywhere near as long as it was. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man. Good job crafting long-winded skip backstory interviews that are actually interesting. I could hear this guy's voice, and he was a hoot! Got really concerned for him in the middle there. And good lord, can the upside-down faced man get any creepier?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did, indeed! Boy, Manny was furious. Not as furious as he was after the ol' noodle incident of course, but you know.

So does the Circus also contain a blonde-haired reality bender and a sapient bipedal tiger?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the other hand, it's equally common for someone to say that they downvoted because the effects weren't interesting enough, even if the story was nice.

While I do understand your concern, I believed it was the story that had to be interesting to get the positive feedback, not the anomaly in an SCP article. Then again, I'm relatively new around here.

And, by the way, loved this article. It feels like a tale, but it's written in such a way that it fits in the format perfectly, and it is a good story. Particularly interested in the fact that the "freaks" have a boogie man, and it was Marshall, Carter and Dark… and then it was the Foundation.

Upvoted. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that there seems to be a bit of a double standard when it comes to what people ask for in an SCP. On the one hand, it's an oft-repeated piece of advice that the writer's focus should always be on the story of the SCP, not on the effects. On the other hand, it's equally common for someone to say that they downvoted because the effects weren't interesting enough, even if the story was nice.

That's what inspired this piece. The anomalous effects are fairly unremarkable, comparatively speaking, but you get the guy's life story and learn all the creative uses he found for his abilities throughout the years.

Also of note: even though Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting has grown into a full-blown GoI, it was originally envisioned as a limited set of interconnected articles. Folks are still welcome to contribute as they like, but as far as my stuff goes, this is number 5 of 7 in the series. There will be two more after this, and then I'll be done with the Circus.

Act 1, Act 2, Act 3, Act 4, Act 5.

If you want more Herman Fuller, check out the awesome stuff that that Those Other Guys put together for the GoI contest! I'm a big fan of their additions to the Circus mythos, and careful observers will observe a nod to one of their tales in this piece.

Discover more of my weird ramblings in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~
